entry.title: S/4HANA Output Management vs. NAST: When to Choose Which?
entry.content:
As SAP consultants, we often find ourselves standing at the crossroads of technology, gazing into the abyss of a decision. One road leads to the shiny, new world of S/4HANA Output Management with its fancy BRF+ engine 🚀, and the other? Well, it’s the path to the good ol’ reliable NAST, the grandpa of output management 👴. But just like choosing between a Tesla and your trusty 1998 Toyota Corolla, the decision isn't always as obvious as you might think. Recently, I was all aboard the S/4HANA Output Management train until it derailed spectacularly, forcing us to make a U-turn back to NAST. Buckle up, SAP adventurers, as we compare these two output options in the wild world of SAP and figure out when to embrace the future—or when to stay loyal to our old friend, NAST.

S/4HANA Output Management: The Bright, Shiny New Thing ✨

S/4HANA Output Management is the new kid on the block, powered by BRF+ (Business Rule Framework Plus). Think of BRF+ like that overachiever in school who could juggle five extracurricular activities while acing calculus. It dynamically handles outputs across processes like procure-to-pay and supports all the cool modern channels—email 📧, XML, EDI, and more—all with a slick Fiori interface. You’ll feel like you’re working in a futuristic office from a sci-fi movie.

Perks of S/4HANA Output Management 🎉

  1. Centralized Management 🗂️
    Imagine managing all your outputs from a single platform—like having a control panel for your spaceship! 🚀 You can streamline oversight and reduce miscommunication, all while feeling like a command center general. “Captain, we’ve got all systems go!”

  2. User-Friendly Fiori Interface 🖥️
    The Fiori interface is so intuitive, it’s like your tech-savvy friend giving you a guided tour of a new city—no maps or GPS needed! 🗺️ Say goodbye to navigating a maze of legacy systems. With Fiori, you’ll feel like you’re gliding through a theme park rather than wrestling with a labyrinth.

  3. Integrated Output Channels 📡
    S/4HANA Output Management comes with multi-channel support that integrates seamlessly with modern communication platforms—like the Swiss Army knife of output delivery! 🛠️ No need for extra tools; it’s ready to deliver real-time outputs faster than you can say “Where’s my coffee?”

  4. Scalability 📈
    As your business grows, this system scales up like a superhero putting on their cape! 🦸‍♂️ Need to accommodate increased output demands? No problem! S/4HANA Output Management flexes its muscles and handles it all without breaking a sweat (or a keyboard).

  5. Future-Proofing 🔮
    With the latest technology at your fingertips, you’ll be ready for whatever the future throws your way—like a cat with nine lives! 🐱 By leveraging cutting-edge solutions, you minimize the risk of obsolescence and keep your business relevant. Who needs a crystal ball when you have S/4HANA Output Management?

But before you get too excited, let's talk about where this whiz kid trips over its untied shoelaces.

Where S/4HANA Output Management Drops the Ball 🤦‍♂️

1. Limited Customization of Forms

You’re cruising along, all pumped up about your shiny new output management system, but then you need to add custom fields to your purchase order (PO). Uh-oh. S/4HANA Output Management is like a strict librarian—everything has to be just so 📚. You’re stuck with pre-delivered service nodes, and trying to sneak in your custom fields feels like asking for the Wi-Fi password in a no-tech zone. Sure, it’s possible with some effort, but you’ll need to break out the BRF+ manual (and maybe some aspirin).

NAST, on the other hand? It's like your cool uncle who lets you customize your ride any way you want. Want to add neon lights and custom rims (read: Smart Forms)? Go ahead! With NAST, you can easily enhance SAPscript or Smart Forms and flexibly bring in those custom fields without a lengthy approval process from the librarian.

2. Complex Extensions for Custom Output Formats

S/4HANA Output Management is great… until your client wants their invoices in a format that looks like it was designed by Picasso 🎨. Good luck extending those Adobe Forms. It’s like trying to add a spoiler to a spaceship—it’s not impossible, but it will take time and might defy physics.

NAST? It's the muscle car of output management: pop the hood, and you can tinker with everything 🔧. Whether you want your forms to look like fine art or just something a little more you, NAST lets you rev up those Smart Forms and SAPscript with ease.

3. Handling Multiple Outputs per Document

S/4HANA Output Management is like the one friend who gets flustered when you try to juggle multiple things 🤹. Need to send different versions of the same invoice to different people? Panic mode activated. Sure, BRF+ can handle it, but expect to be knee-deep in complex rules.

NAST, however, says, "Bring it on!" Want to send one invoice to the client, another to finance, and a third to yourself just because? NAST handles it like a pro, thanks to its easy condition records. No sweat 😅.

4. Flexibility in Condition-Based Output Determination

BRF+ tries its best with rule-based output determination, but sometimes it just doesn’t get the memo 📜. Extending BRF+ to accommodate weird, custom conditions can feel like trying to teach a cat to fetch. Possible, but expect resistance 🐱.

NAST, though? NAST is a dog—it loves fetch, and you can train it to do all sorts of tricks. Want outputs based on complex vendor categories, approval levels, or whether Mercury is in retrograde? NAST’s condition-based framework can handle it all with a wag of its tail 🐶.

Example: When S/4HANA Met Its Match

In one project, we thought we’d impress the client by using S/4HANA Output Management for their purchase orders. It was all smooth sailing until—BAM!—we hit the iceberg of "missing custom fields" ⚓. No amount of BRF+ tweaking could save us. We wasted days trying to bend it to our will, only to realize NAST would have done it in a fraction of the time. So, we jumped back into NAST’s arms, customized the PO with Smart Forms, and sailed off into the sunset 🌅.

S/4HANA Output Management vs. NAST: The Tale of the Tape

Feature S/4HANA Output Management NAST (The Classic)

TechnologyBRF+SAPscript / Smart Forms
Customization FlexibilityLimited by service nodesCustomizable to your heart’s content
Form TypeAdobe FormsSAPscript, Smart Forms
Output TypesPrint, Email, XML, EDIPrint, Email, Fax, EDI
Multiple Outputs per DocumentBRF+ (but expect headaches)Easier management with condition records
Learning CurveSteep—better grab some coffee ☕Familiar, like catching up with an old friend
Centralized ManagementYes—streamlined oversightTraditional, less streamlined
Future-ProofingYes—leverages the latest technologyNo—legacy technology means potential obsolescence
Recommended Use CaseStandard scenarios, new systemsCustom-heavy projects, legacy systems

Choosing the Right Output Path 🛤️

When to Choose S/4HANA Output Management:

  • You’re looking to impress with modern, scalable tech.
  • You want centralized management for oversight and efficiency.
  • Your output requirements are standard and predictable.
  • You like the user-friendly Fiori interface and want to leverage integrated output channels.

When to Choose NAST:

  • You have a quirky project with unique needs—custom fields, complex conditions, or multiple output formats.
  • You like the comfort of tried-and-true technology.
  • You enjoy the flexibility and ease of SAPscript and Smart Forms, like an old pair of comfy slippers 🥿.

Conclusion

S/4HANA Output Management is the sleek sports car of output systems—modern, fast, and looks great in the showroom 🚗. But sometimes, when you need to haul a weird-shaped load (read: custom fields and complex outputs), you’ll find that trusty NAST still has plenty of mileage left in the tank. So, whether you're cruising into the future or sticking with the classics, just remember: it’s not about the tech—it’s about what gets you to the finish line with fewer headaches 🎉.

entry.title: Cloud ERP two-tier Confluence Event Highlights – Blog collection
entry.content:
We are excited to share highlights of Cloud ERP Two-tier Confluence Event through this blog series.

Reflecting on the event, we delved into an overview, master data integration, various sales office scenarios and recent innovations. This blog series aims to help users better understand Two-tier ERP with  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition  

Two-tier ERP addresses groups of companies including headquarters and subsidiaries. It comprises a headquarters ERP with high landscape complexity and being highly customized, and a subsidiaries ERP to support quick scale at lower costs with low landscape complexity. Both headquarters and subsidiaries ERP are integrated in a hybrid landscape with suitable implementation and process optimizations

Check below blog links to know updates from each of the areas

  • Two-tier ERP Overview
  • Two-tier ERP Master Data Replication Options
  • Two-tier ERP Transactional Data Replication options 
  • Sales Office scenarios in Two-tier ERP 
  • Two-tier ERP with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408

To know more click on " What is two-tier ERP? " page on sap.com

You can also reach out two-tier ERP community for more clarifications

Stay tuned for in-depth posts on each topic

entry.title: Sustainability in Defense and Security: Why It’s Essential
entry.content:
Why Sustainability Matters

 

“We simply must do everything we can in our power to slow down global warming before it is too late. We can save our planet and also boost our economy at the same time.”

 

You may now be wondering what this has to do with Defense and Security organizations such as armed forces, firefighters, police, ambulance services, or aid organizations. The answer is simply more than most people think. For one thing, according to the leading consultancy McKinsey, D&S organizations account for approximately 50% of governments’ carbon emissions from fleets and facilities in countries like Canada and the United Kingdom, and up to about 80% in the United States of America. On the other hand, if you consider global CO2 emissions, the military sector accounts for 1-5%. Although this stake does not seem high at first glance, it certainly does when you set it in relation to other business sectors and see that the emissions are comparably high as in the aviation or shipping industry. This means that any government that aims to reduce its overall emissions needs to consider its defense forces in its climate action plan too. For another thing, current, state-of-the-art sustainability solutions do not really harm the organizational performance or the practical operational readiness of military organizations anymore. This is especially because key processes, such as the measurement of emissions, are usually run in the background through automated calculations and the power of highly innovative sensor technology and software. But more on that later. Before that, I want to inform you about some additional statistical data and facts about sustainability. The consulting company Boston Consulting Group (BCG) found that the global defense industry’s share in global CO2 emissions could rise significantly from 2% in 2021 to 25% in 2050 if the industry does not take active action to reduce its emissions. But what do these emissions look like in detail?

 

Figure 1: CO2 Emissions of the US Department of Defense (2023)

 

As the above pie chart of the CO2 emissions of the US Department of Defense in 2023 shows, most emissions result from vehicles and equipment or purchased electricity. In Germany, however, where the emissions of the Bundeswehr increased by 17.9% in the period of 2019 to 2021, we see a somewhat different picture since most of the increase results from increased fuel consumption of the air force as well as increased heating requirements of the Bundeswehr’s military infrastructure. Interestingly, as the well-known Nature magazine pointed out in an article, many military organizations around the world still do not measure and report their emissions in as much detail as other governmental organizations or private companies do. In contrast, 95% of the largest companies globally publish ESG (environmental, social, and governance) reports regularly.

 

What Actions are Required to meet the Climate Targets?

 

However, although the above statistics may not look really promising to the reader, we luckily see that the problem was recognized a long time ago by many Defense & Security organizations. Military organizations such as the US Department of Defense, the German Bundeswehr, or NATO have actively created climate action plans within the last few years:

 

The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in this regard set itself the following targets in 2021:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 45% by 2030.
  • Achievement of net zero emissions (Net zero emissions occur when the amount of greenhouse gases emitted equals the amount removed from the atmosphere) by 2050.

 

The European Union, on the other hand, has set the defense sector within the Union similar climate action goals:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55% by 2030 compared to 1990 levels.
  • Carbon neutrality by 2050.
  • Increased use of renewable energies and focus on improvement of energy efficiency within military operations.
  • Implementation of sustainable practices in defense operations.
  • Passing of a so-called “Climate-Security Nexus”, that addresses the link between climate change and security threats, thereby including climate considerations into defense planning and operations.

 

As can be seen in the text above, there are already a number of promising strategic plans, that include proven concepts and best practices for reducing greenhouse gas emissions of military organizations. However, specialized management consultancies such as BCG and McKinsey in this regard have drawn up a number of additional recommendations for making defense organizations more sustainable:

 

Recommendations from McKinsey

McKinsey suggests leveraging low-carbon technologies by using electric vehicles, sustainable aviation fuel, and renewable energy sources. Additionally, the consultancy recommends putting the focus mainly on non-mission-critical areas. This according to McKinsey allows armed forces organizations to achieve a 60% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions. Other suggestions from the company include to establish an end-to-end consideration of sustainability in value chains and supply chains of Defense and Security organizations.

 

Recommendations from the Boston Consulting Group:

The US-based consulting company Boston Consulting Company (BCG) principally makes similar suggestions. It’s main recommendation is to make significant investments into sustainable technologies through research and development. Furthermore, it highly recommends D&S organizations to enhance emissions reporting and accountability as well as to pursue net-zero emission goals.

 

Other Recommendations and Initiatives:

The consulting company KPMG suggests D&S organizations to align their climate goals and actions with international and national climate targets, whereas Nature advocates for mandatory emissions reporting and international agreements and methodologies to monitor and reduce military emissions. Additionally, D&S organizations such as the US DoD or the US Army have established their own guidelines for water efficiency and water security.

 

Sustainability Targets of Emergency Organizations:

These recommendations are not only true for military organizations, but are applicable to emergency organizations like Police, Ambulances or Firefighting organizations in a similar manner. Examples include organizations such as the London Fire Brigade, which has outlined a “Sustainable Development Strategy for 2023-2025” and consequently invested in measures like acquiring electric vehicles and making their fire stations more energy efficient. Other examples in this direction include the Berlin Police, that has implemented measures such as using hybrid and electric cars along with sustainable practices such as a focus on recycling and a reduction of paper use. In doing so the emergency organization aims to support the City of Berlin in reaching its climate reduction 2030 and climate neutrality 2045 goals.

 

Given the above-mentioned challenges and facts, I now want to draw the reader’s attention to the holistic SAP Sustainability Solutions, that allow D&S organizations to actively tackle and manage all common aspects of sustainability.  

 

The SAP Sustainability Solutions

 

 

Video 1: ERP to ESG: How to develop your first ESG Strategy

 

SAP is proud to be one of the leading software suppliers for Enterprise Resource Planning and other software solutions for various leading companies and organizations around the world. However, what is less well-known across customers and industries is that SAP also offers powerful and holistic cross-industry sustainability software solutions. The leading technology research companies, Gartner and IDC, have recognized SAP’s sustainability solutions for their comprehensive capabilities. Gartner has rated SAP as a preferred vendor for carbon accounting and management software solutions, while IDC has praised the “breadth and depth” of SAP’s offerings. The SAP solutions are thereby not only highly capable of measuring, analyzing, and managing carbon emissions, but are also trusted by leading companies and organizations worldwide, such as IBM, PWC, Freudenberg, msg global, and SAP itself. SAP’s sustainability solutions feature real-time data analytics, seamless integration with existing ERP systems, and AI-driven insights to help businesses make informed decisions. For example, IBM has leveraged SAP’s solutions to significantly reduce its carbon footprint, while PWC has used them to enhance its sustainability reporting. SAP’s commitment to sustainability has also been recognized with several industry awards, and the company continues to innovate with new features and developments on the horizon.

In general, the SAP Sustainability Cloud software suite is built on the following four main software solutions:

 

1) Sustainability and ESG Reporting Software 

 

Video 2: SAP Sustainability Control Tower – Holistic Steering and ESG Reporting

 

Figure 2: The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution

 

SAP allows companies and organizations to connect their environmental, social, and governance (ESG) data and steer their organizations towards more sustainable and efficient operations, thereby also helping to achieve national and international climate goals. The SAP ESG software was designed in a holistic way and provides streamlined reporting as well as valuable insights for decision-makers and stakeholders. Furthermore, it allows for the full integration of the organization’s stakeholders, like suppliers, into the system. This also means that governments theoretically can implement the solution nation-wide or across different governmental areas for managing everything around sustainability with one central software platform. Apart from that, the software generally allows D&S organizations to measure, communicate, and achieve corporate sustainability goals to the broader public. Moreover, the sustainability software is integrable across various different systems, even across the respective organization or company. This allows bringing sustainability, safety, risk, compliance, asset, and people data together in one place for enabling comprehensive sustainability reporting and analysis.

 

2) SAP Climate Action Solutions

 

Video 3: Transactional Carbon Accounting with SAP

 

Figure 3: Measuring emissions with the SAP Sustainability Management solutions

 

The SAP Climate Action Solutions allow companies and organizations to decarbonize their entire value chain from end-to-end to become climate-neutral organizations. This is made possible by integrating actual business transactions with carbon accounting software, emissions and compliance management, carbon footprint calculations, e-mobility, and transportation logistics. The solution includes services like:

  • Real-time decision making with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management
  • SAP Sustainability Data Exchange
  • SAP Environment, Health and Safety Management
  • and many more services

 

3) Circular Economy Software 

 

Video 4: Circular Design with SAP

 

Figure 4: The SAP Circular Economy solutions

 

The SAP Circular Economy solutions allow companies and organizations to integrate the concept of circularity into their organization. Circularity thereby allows them to reduce the use of resources and materials, such as plastic, as much as possible. In addition, the concept aims to eliminate waste and circulate materials. Our Circular Economy solutions thereby consider all these requirements and allow managing circularity holistically. This includes circularity in supply chains, production, and all other applicable organizational processes. Especially worth mentioning in this regard is the possibility to use blockchain technology for capturing the whole material provenance from raw materials to finished products. Other promising features of the circular economy solutions are, for instance, the ability to create circular logistic flows for transport containers, pallets, and other forms of packaging.

 

4) Social Responsibility Solutions

 

Video 5: Social Responsibility for Zero Inequality with SAP

 

Figure 5: The EcoVadis API Connector for SAP Ariba

 

The SAP Social Responsibility solutions allow companies and organizations to manage their impact on people and society centrally with one software package. This involves the ability to consider social and environmental requirements across the entire value and supply chains of your organization. Moreover, it encompasses the ability to proactively ensure health, safety, as well as labor rights within the entire organization, but also to establish workforce sustainability by creating equal treatment and opportunities like, for instance, guaranteeing equal pay for men and women. In addition, SAP within this thematic area offers D&S organizations a human rights due diligence software that allows them to enforce human rights across all supply chains.

 

Artificial Intelligence Functions and Features

 

SAP is planning to integrate Artificial Intelligence into all eligible software solutions. For instance, it has set a goal to embed its Generative AI-powered Joule Copilot into 80% of the most used tasks by the end of 2024. However, various super-helpful AI solutions are already available, including those for SAP Sustainability Management solutions, where our customers can benefit from:

 

- AI for Emission Factor Management

(Demo Video: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Features)

Our customers can simplify emission factor management by using the SAP Business AI-powered automation function, which is directly integrated into the solution. This improves result accuracy and reduces manual labor.

 

- AI for ESG Report Generation

The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution allows users to record, report, and act on their diverse sustainability data. SAP has recently enhanced this solution by integrating an AI feature for the automated creation of ESG reports. This streamlines the reporting process, saving valuable time and money.

 

 

The Security Architecture of the SAP Sustainability Management Solutions

 

The SAP Sustainability Management software suite is built on an end-to-end security architecture that meets the demands of even the most security-conscious companies and organizations around the world. This includes:

 

- Cloud-Based Security

The sustainability software solutions are built with a security-first cloud approach. The cloud solution is based on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), which includes strict functionalities for access control and data encryption. Additionally, SAP continuously develops its products and distributes regular software updates and upgrades to its clients.

 

- Data Integration and Auditing

SAP Sustainability Management is directly integrated or integrable into core Enterprise Resource Planning processes, making all data directly traceable and auditable.

 

- AI-Enabled Automation

Artificial Intelligence technology plays an important role through the SAP Joule Copilot and various embedded AI functions within SAP applications. It is also crucial for the security of the SAP Sustainability Management solutions. AI is used for process automation, eliminating the risk of human error, and for continuous monitoring of data and processes. If the system detects any security threat, it will directly inform the system user, who can then investigate and resolve the issue.

 

- Flexible and Extensible Architecture

SAP Sustainability Control Tower, one of the core sustainability solutions, offers clients maximum flexibility by allowing them to integrate custom content and create customized solutions.

 

- Integration of Regulatory Standards for Compliance

The SAP Sustainability Management solutions allow the incorporation of all major regulatory frameworks and legal requirements into the system. Examples for this include for instance the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). Defense and Security organizations can also integrate their own sustainability guidelines, such as national and international standards or guidelines from defense organizations like NATO.

 

 

Conclusion

 

In conclusion, this blog entry has shown that sustainability is crucial for Defense and Security (D&S) organizations, not only for achieving organizational, national, or international climate goals but also for creating significant opportunities for organizational realignment towards a greener orientation. By adopting sustainable practices, D&S organizations can contribute to combating global warming while simultaneously saving significant costs on fuel consumption, heating systems, and weapon systems, without sacrificing their core defense capabilities. It is important to note the significant positive influence such transformations can have on public perception and the innovative strength of individual military organizations and the entire D&S industry. A good example is the automotive industry, which, like many other industries, has undergone significant transformation in recent years with the shift to electric drives, now offering more modern and technologically advanced products, that are also more environmentally friendly. António Guterres, the Secretary-General of the United Nations, aptly stated, “The climate emergency is a race we are losing, but it is a race we can win.” This call to action resonates deeply within the defense sector, urging all stakeholders to embrace sustainable practices and technologies. However, setting climate goals and creating corresponding guidelines is one thing, but achieving them another. That is why D&S organizations need to develop holistic sustainability concepts in partnership with key stakeholders, such as governments, suppliers, defense organizations, or supranational organizations like the European Union. They also need to measure, manage, and report their progress. This is where SAP’s cutting-edge SAP Sustainability Management solutions come into play. The software package covers all typical sustainability requirements for D&S organizations and includes various supplementary tools and features, such as ESG reporting, circular economy solutions, social responsibility, health and safety solutions, and innovative AI solutions. In conclusion, by establishing climate and sustainability action plans and utilizing powerful sustainability management solutions like SAP Sustainability Management, D&S organizations can set a powerful example, proving that security and sustainability can go hand in hand. Let’s embark on this journey together towards a greener, more sustainable future for everyone.

 

 

Sources

 

Berlin Energy and Climate Protection Programme 2030. (2018, January, 25). Berlin.de.

Climate change considerations for EU. (2022, June). European Parliament.

Climate-Neutral Berlin 2045. (2021, August). Berlin.de.

Decarbonizing defense carbon emissions . (2021, July, 01). McKinsey.

SAP Sustainability Control Tower Update: Holistic ESG . (2022, November). SAP News.

Schwarzenegger takes center stage on warming. (2006, September, 27). NBC News.

entry.title: Visualise end to end progress of Solution using Solution Quotation Fiori application
entry.content:
In today's fast-paced business environment, visibility and traceability are critical for effective decision-making. SAP’s Manage Solution Quotation Version 2 app addresses this need by offering a comprehensive view of the entire solution from creation of solution quotation to porgress of each follow-up documents execution. This blog highlighting the new feature and how businesses can leverage it to optimize operations.

From 2023 FPS01, new fiori app 'Manage Solution Quotations - Version 2' is availalbe to use. Using this app user can easily create new solution quotations or search or navigate to existing solution quotations. This app provides a network graph view, allowing user to track the status of a predefined set of follow-up documents such as sales orders, service orders, service contract or subscription contract. At each graphical node, like a sales order or subscription contract, you can see key details including the document ID and status with colour coded node of each follow-ups. A focused progress monitor is available at both the solution quotation header and item levels, offering a clear view of the overall process. Users can also navigate directly to specific documents or transactions, provided they have the necessary roles for access.

On open of this app you will see list of solution quotations created, user can search using the default filters, also can adapt the given filters.

To navigate to the details page of a solution quotation, click on the respective row. To visualize the end-to-end progress of the solution, user must click the "Action" button in the corresponding solution quotation row. On click on action button it opens the fiori object page with Details, Items and Progress Monitor. In this example I have used mixed bundled product which is a combination of different configurable non configurable products. Solution Quotation has been accepted which has generated its follow-up documents( sales order, service order, service contract and subscription contract).

By default, it shows the progress flow of the header documents as depicted in the image above. To visualize a specific item, click on the corresponding line item, To illustrate in this example I have selected item 40, which is a subscription item.

As you progress with specific documents, such as sales documents, you can visualize the further documents like outbound delivery and invoice. For example I have created billing documents for Subscription item and Service Contract, user can visualise end to end progress of header and items. 

Tips:The Progress Monitor UI offers useful controls in the right corner. Users can zoom in or zoom out, and clicking on 'Enter Full Screen' will expand the view for better visualization. Users can also highlight a particular document's flow by clicking on the document (node), as shown in the image below.

Another useful control is 'Expand All' and 'Collapse All.' As your document set grows, you can collapse all and expand only the documents you want to track. For example, here I have collapsed all documents and expanded the Service Contract flow.

Navigate to Documents from Progress Monitor: To navigate, the user can click on a node, which will display a set of buttons. By clicking the "Navigate to Document" button, the available navigation options(hyperlinks) will be shown depending on the user's authorizations. On click on hyperlink you can navigate from progress monitor to correponding documents. Similalry you can explore other interactive feature by clicking on button like Expand/Collapse, Node Details and Collapse Path.

Navigation of Sales Documents 

In summary, the SAP  Manage Solution Quotation version 2 app offers a user-friendly interface for managing and tracking solution quotations. The SAP solution quotation progress monitor enhances business efficiency by providing full visibility into the solution, enabling faster decision-making and improved collaboration. This streamlined process helps organizations reduce errors, accelerate decision making, and optimize resource allocation, driving overall business growth.

Stay tune for my next blog post I will try to explain the other features of Solution Business. I will be grateful to see your feedback on this blog post also let me know if you expect specific blog post on other offering of Manage Solution Quotation application or Solution Business.

entry.title: Choosing the Right Path for SAP S/4HANA Transformation: Greenfield, Brownfield, or Bluefield?
entry.content:
transformation involves several key steps

  1. System Conversion: Moving from the old SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA, which requires a technical upgrade and data migration.
  2. New Implementation: Implementing SAP S/4HANA from scratch for organizations starting fresh or restructuring their business processes.
  3. Selective Data Transition: Combining elements of both system conversion and new implementation, selecting specific data or processes to move.

The transformation offers improved performance, real-time analytics, and simplified IT landscapes due to S/4HANA’s in-memory database and modern user experience through Fiori.

 

Greenfield Implementation                  Brownfield Implementation                 Bluefield Implementation

A fresh, new implementation of SAP S/4HANA from scratch.A system conversion that migrates an existing SAP ERP system to S/4HANA.A hybrid approach that blends Greenfield and Brownfield strategies.
Used when starting with new processes or overhauling old systems.Keeps existing customizations, configurations, and processes while upgrading to S/4HANA.Selectively retains and transforms existing processes and data.
Ideal for companies looking for full transformation.Suitable for organizations aiming to keep most of their current system setup.Often used to minimize risk, time, and cost by combining the best of both worlds.

comparing the pros and cons of Greenfield, Brownfield, and Bluefield implementation in SAP:

Approach             Pros                                                                       Cons

Greenfield- Complete process redesign and optimization.
- Full flexibility in implementing best practices.
- No legacy system constraints.		- Time-consuming.
- Higher costs due to full rebuild.
- Requires significant change management.
Brownfield- Faster implementation.
- Retains existing customizations and processes.
- Less disruption to current operations.		- Limited flexibility to adopt new processes.
- Potential migration of inefficiencies.
- May require extensive technical adjustments.
Bluefield- Combines benefits of Greenfield and Brownfield.
- Selective process and data transformation.
- Reduces cost and risk of full rebuild.		- Can be complex to manage.
- Requires careful planning to balance old and new.
- May not achieve full innovation like Greenfield.

 

I tried to keep this article simple and high-level, making it easy for everyone from novices to experts.

entry.title: The Roadmap to Success: Leveraging Best Practices and Activate Methodology for S/4HANA Implementatio
entry.content:
Navigating the path to a successful S/4HANA implementation may be daunting, but by leveraging best practices and SAP’s Activate methodology, businesses can streamline the process and gain the most value from their investment. In this post, we’ll explore how to make the most of scope items, ready-to-run business processes, documentation, and tools like the Signavio Process Navigator.

Understanding the SAP Activate Methodology

The SAP Activate methodology is a unique, modular, and agile framework that guides you through the transformation process. It encompasses four stages: Prepare, Explore, Realize, and Deploy. The methodology offers a roadmap, providing businesses with step-by-step guidance, accelerators, and best practices to achieve a smooth implementation.

 

 

In this blogpost we will mainly focus on the power of the best practices.

The Power of Best Practices

 

In the S/4HANA implementation journey, SAP’s best practices are crucial. These pre-packaged, ready-to-run business processes encapsulate the collective wisdom of SAP experts acquired from a myriad of successful projects. Each is detailed in scope items, each representing a unique business scenario replete with associated business processes and configuration settings.

These best practices come with comprehensive documentation, such as process diagrams, configuration guides, and data loading procedures, providing your team with a wealth of invaluable information to leverage. By using these best practices, you can speed up your S/4HANA implementation, reduce risks, and ensure alignment with tested strategies.

In the continuous spirit of innovation and to further enhance user experience, SAP has introduced the new Process Navigator, designed to replace the Best Practice Explorer. This cutting-edge tool promises to revolutionize how businesses access and utilize best practices.

The Process Navigator provides a more streamlined, intuitive, and interactive experience, allowing users to navigate through various processes and scope items in a more efficient and organized manner. It offers greater visibility into processes, helping users understand the context, interactions, and the flow of business processes better.

It also enhances the way you can search for and access documentation related to different scope items and processes. This ensures a more integrated, holistic view of best practices, promoting a more efficient and effective S/4HANA implementation process.

 

As you transition to using the new Process Navigator, remember that it’s designed with the user in mind. It combines the powerful functionalities of the Best Practice Explorer with a more user-friendly interface and additional capabilities, promising a more seamless experience in leveraging best practices for your S/4HANA implementation.

 

Making the Most of Scope Items and Leveraging the Solution Builder

Scope items are pivotal to unlocking the power of SAP’s best practices. Each scope item is a ready-to-use business process that can be activated straight away, with relevant software requirements, implementation efforts, necessary data, and configuration guidelines detailed within.

A key tool to fully utilize scope items is the SAP Solution Builder. This robust tool is instrumental in aiding organizations to create a structured and tailored implementation guide based on SAP’s best practices. It allows the selection of the required scope items and generates a pre-configured solution that caters to your business requirements.

Moreover, Solution Builder allows organizations to customize scope items and add new ones, offering flexibility in shaping the solution to meet specific business needs. This capability ensures that organizations can adjust the pre-configured solutions to match their processes, leading to increased efficiency and productivity.

 

One of the important steps in making the most of scope items and the Solution Builder is to understand your business needs fully. By comprehending your business’s requirements, processes, and needs, you can identify the most relevant scope items for your organization. This understanding will then guide your mapping of the implementation plan, backed by SAP’s extensive experience and best practices.

Lastly, remember that there are comprehensive step-by-step guides available online to aid you in leveraging the Solution Builder and implementing scope items. These guides provide in-depth explanations, screenshots, and practical tips to assist your team during the implementation process. These resources can be instrumental in avoiding common pitfalls and ensuring a smooth and successful implementation of S/4HANA. Here is a link to the awsome blog series from Mahesh Sardesai: New Installation of SAP S/4HANA 2022 FPS1 – Part 3 – Best Practices Content Activation | SAP Blogs. I’m folowwing his step by step guides eversince they started publishing it.

 

Documentation: The Backbone of your Implementation

Thorough and accurate documentation is key to ensuring a smooth and successful S/4HANA implementation. Documentation within scope items provides detailed insights into the steps required for implementing each business process, configuration guides for each scenario, test scripts for validation, and procedures for data loading.

Maintaining an updated and comprehensive documentation set will help your implementation team understand the processes and guide them through each stage. It also provides a reference for future troubleshooting and audits, allowing for continuous improvement and upkeep.

 

 

 

Streamlining your Implementation with Signavio Process Navigator

One of the challenges during an S/4HANA implementation is managing and understanding the multitude of business processes and their interactions. This is where the Signavio Process Navigator comes in. This powerful tool allows you to map, manage, and optimize your business processes, providing a clear visual representation of your process landscape.

It facilitates an end-to-end perspective of your processes, helping to identify potential bottlenecks, inefficiencies, or areas that require improvement. Moreover, the tool can simulate various scenarios, enabling you to test and refine your processes before going live. By incorporating the Signavio Process Navigator into your implementation plan, you can ensure that your S/4HANA implementation aligns perfectly with your business operations and objectives.

 

Conclusion

Leveraging SAP’s best practices and the Activate methodology, coupled with a clear understanding of scope items, comprehensive documentation, and tools like the Signavio Process Navigator, can significantly streamline your S/4HANA implementation. This approach not only mitigates risks but also ensures that your organization reaps the full benefits of your S/4HANA. Providing you a jumpstart of your S/4 Journey.

SAP Signavio Process Navigator SAP Signavio Process Navigator

entry.content:
Dear Learners,

I am glad to inform you that we have recently been published on SAP Podcasts  

SAP Learning Insights Clean Core with Mark Quincy Anderson Focus: Clean Core and Cloud ERP

In this episode of SAP Learning Insights, David Chaviano is joined by Quincy Anderson,  product learning expert at SAP, to delve into the intricacies of the concept of Clean Core, SAP ERP systems, particularly focusing on the transformative shift from on-premise to cloud-based solutions.
Central to the discussion is the Clean Core philosophy which ensures that any customisations or extensions made by companies on their SAP ERP systems do not obstruct the seamless integration of new updates - fostering a stable, yet flexible digital backbone compatible with continuous scalability and quick adaptation to market changes.
Clean Core in a Cloud ERP environment isn't just an IT strategy, but a pivotal business decision ensuring sustained competitive advantage in a rapidly evolving digital landscape.

entry.title: Cycle Counting Physical Inventory in SAP WM (Stock Room Management) at Bin Level
entry.content:
Introduction- In cycle counting inventory, you enter your material stocks physically at regular intervals within a fiscal year. By dividing materials into various classes (for example, A, B, C, D, and so on), you specify for those materials at which intervals or cycles the inventory is to be performed. This allows you to make an inventory fast-moving materials in your warehouse more often than slow-moving material.

Cycle counting is basically the process of continually validation the accuracy of the inventory in the warehouse by regularly counting a portion of the inventory on a daily or weekly basis. Every item in the warehouse is counted at lease several times in a year.

Cycle Counting can be performed at IM level as well as WM Level.

In WM  We can go for two types of Cycle Counting Physical Inventory

1- Cycle Counting at Bin Level

2- Cycle Counting at Quant Level 

In this blog I am going to write about Cycle Counting at Bin Level

WM Level Cycle Counting Configuration

  • ABC  indicator has to be maintained in Material Management

Navigation SPRO-Material Management-Inventory Management and Physical Inventory-Physical Inventory-Cycle Counting

Master Data Set UP at IM Level

Set Cycle Counting Indicator (M1BC)

Navigation Logistics – Material Management- Physical Inventory- Special Procedures-Cycle Counting – Set Cycle Counting Indicator

Click change CC Indicator

It can be checked in MM03

 

Cycle Counting Configuration in WM for Storage Type

Navigation SPRO-Logistic Execution- Warehouse Management – Activity – Physical Inventory -Define Types per storage type

Process Flow

Create Cycle Counting Document in WM

Transaction Code LX26

Count the inventory

Transaction code – LI11n

Input Counted quantity In put here lesser than actual

And save the count

Inventory Count Added

Clear difference in WM

Transaction LI20

Write Off the count

Clear difference at IM level

Transaction code LI21 or LX23

I am taking here LI21

After write off  Material document will be created

Check LX03

One pallet quantity was 0 so it disappeared

Other pallet quantity 1 and it was increased by 1 quantity so it is changed

After the entire physical inventory is complete for the fiscal year, you reset the inventory data for storage bins and storage quants using the RLREOLPQ report. In the next inventory period, you can then perform a new physical inventory for these storage bins.

Note- You should not reset the quant data, since the reference to the inventory date is then lost, and the system proposes too many bins for your next cycle counting inventory.

Reference - https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/b2dee5e83e2446149294f9860a7c08f0/8f91c95360267214e10000000a174cb4.html

Please share your feedback so I can improve it 

 

Regards

Rashid

entry.title: Cycle Counting Physical Inventory in SAP WM(Stock Room Management) at Quant Level
entry.content:
Hi All

I wrote blog previously about Cycle Counting at Bin Level https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/cycle-counting-physical-inventory-in-sap-wm-at-bin-level/ba-p/13938236. In this blog I am going to write about Cycle Counting at Quant Level in WM.

Introduction- In contrast to the Cycle Counting Inventory, the cycle counting inventory at quant level is not based on the storage bin, but rather on the individual quants in your warehouse.

If you use cycle counting inventory at quant level, the system does not set the inventory block for the entire storage bin. It sets the block instead for the quant to be inventoried. You count only the selected materials, not all materials in the storage bin. If you manage storage bins with mixed storage, these storage bins are accessible for stock movements with other quants. There can be open transfer orders for all materials that were not flagged for cycle counting in the inventory document. You can also create transfer orders for these materials during the inventory. The system saves the inventory data at quant level.

All Configuration Steps will be same I mentioned in my Previous Blog https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/cycle-counting-physical-inventory-in-sap-wm-at-bin-level/ba-p/13938236 Except below additional configuration,

In Customizing - SPRO- Logistic Execution-Warehouse Management- Activities- Physical Inventory- Define Types per Storage Type ,

 

you have specified the inventory procedure for the required storage type as continuous inventory using cycle counting.

 

SPRO-Logistic Execution- Warehouse Management -Activities- Physical Inventory -Maintain Number Ranges for Inventory number range Quant

Process Step

To create physical inventory documents at quant level for the cycle counting inventory

Logistics Execution- Internal Whse Processes - Physical Inventory - In Warehouse Management Physical Inventory Document - Cycle Counting per Quant
Transaction Code LICC

Note- If you generate inventory documents for the cycle counting inventory at quant level, these documents are active immediately . In this case, you cannot schedule the physical inventory in advance by first generating the inventory documents and activating them at a later point.The system immediately blocks the affected quants for all other stock movements.

For Rest Process Step we can refer last Blog https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/cycle-counting-physical-inventory-in-sap-wm-at-bin-level/ba-p/13938236

Count LI11N

WM Level Difference LI20

IM Level Difference LI21 or LX23

To obtain an evaluation about the progress of the cycle counting

Logistics Execution  Internal Whse Processes  Information System  Warehouse  Physical Inventory  With Bin Inventory Management   Evaluation of Quant Inventory
Transaction Code LI15

Note- In a stock movement, if you move a quant from one storage bin to another, the system deletes the quant in the source storage bin and creates a new quant in the destination storage bin. Because the system stores the inventory data for the cycle counting inventory at quant level in the quant data, any inventory data that you previously entered is lost in this type of stock transfer.

Conclusion- This method is very useful. we dont want to block all stock or bin for a particular material so we can use it by blocking a quant. It will help us to run business smoothly without impacting.

Please share your feedback to improve it 

References- https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/b2dee5e83e2446149294f9860a7c08f0/278ec95360267214e10000000a174cb4.html

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/cycle-counting-physical-inventory-in-sap-wm-at-bin-level/ba-p/13938236 

 

Thanks
